name: Crypto AG
description: Crypto AG was a Swiss encryption company secretly owned by the CIA and West German intelligence (BND) from the 1970s until the early 1990s. This arrangement, codenamed 'Operation Rubicon', allowed these intelligence agencies to eavesdrop on the communications of over 120 countries, including allies and adversaries. The company sold encryption devices to governments worldwide, unaware that the devices were deliberately weakened to allow the CIA and BND to break the codes. Proponents argue this manipulation represents a massive breach of trust and a violation of national sovereignty, allowing the US and West Germany to gain significant geopolitical advantages. The mainstream perspective acknowledges the accuracy of the reports confirming Operation Rubicon, based on declassified documents and investigative journalism. However, debate continues regarding the long-term impact and ethical implications of the operation. Some argue that it was a necessary evil during the Cold War, while others condemn it as an act of international espionage that undermined global security and trust. The Swiss government conducted an investigation and concluded that Swiss authorities were likely aware of the CIA's involvement and that the company's activities had violated Swiss neutrality. The case raises important questions about the role of private companies in intelligence operations and the balance between national security and individual privacy.
subtitle: Secret CIA-Controlled Encryption Company

claims.claim: Crypto AG was secretly owned by the CIA and BND.
claims.evidence: Declassified CIA and BND documents, investigative reporting by the Washington Post and ZDF.
claims.criticism: Initially denied by involved parties, but later confirmed through credible evidence.

claims.claim: Crypto AG's encryption devices were deliberately weakened.
claims.evidence: Internal Crypto AG documents, testimonies from former employees, analysis of the encryption algorithms.
claims.criticism: While technical details remain classified, independent cryptographers have confirmed the feasibility and likelihood of such manipulation.

claims.claim: Operation Rubicon provided significant geopolitical advantages to the US and West Germany.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal evidence from intercepted communications, improved intelligence gathering capabilities, strategic decision-making based on eavesdropped information.
claims.criticism: Difficult to quantify the exact impact, but the scale of the operation suggests a substantial advantage in intelligence gathering and strategic planning.

keyFigures.name: Wilhelm Agrell
keyFigures.role: Historian and security policy expert who analyzed the affair.

keyFigures.name: Allen Dulles
keyFigures.role: Former Director of Central Intelligence; likely involved in the early stages of the agreement.

keyFigures.name: Reinhard Gehlen
keyFigures.role: Head of the BND, involved in the agreement with the CIA.

mythologyConnections: The concept of Trojan Horses, representing hidden threats within trusted systems.

timeline.event: Initial discussions between the CIA and Crypto AG founder Boris Hagelin.
Formal agreement between the CIA and BND to acquire Crypto AG.
The BND withdraws from the operation.
Public revelations of Operation Rubicon through investigative journalism.

sources.author: Greg Miller
sources.work: U.S. Spy Agencies Used Swiss Company as Trojan Horse
sources.title: Greg Miller

sources.author: Herbert Romerstein
sources.work: The Venona Secrets: Exposing Soviet Espionage and America's Traitors
sources.title: Herbert Romerstein
